caseInventory: The live pack TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES (lib/benchmark/toolreliability/cases.ts:161-168) has 125 cases, all generated from Array.from templates: 15 json-schema (3 schemas x 5 identical prompts), 25 tool-call (1 echo template in 2 skins), 15 basic patch (1 two-line-file template), 50 large-file patch (5 kinds x 10 size variants), 10 repair-loop (2 schemas x 5), 10 forbidden-action (10 byte-identical prompts). It is reachable at runtime via components/benchmark/certified/CertifiedRunPanel.tsx:563 (certified toolreliability track) and :802 (TeamIQ full pack), plus a 6-case quick subset TEAMIQ_TOOL_RELIABILITY_QUICK_CASES at :797. A further 70 stress cases (50 large-file patch stress + 20 tool-strategy) in stress-cases.ts and their evaluator (stress-evaluator.ts) are exported but consumed only by test scripts (scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts, test-toolreliability-current-names.mts) — never reachable from any UI or certified runner. Counting distinct decision points, the 125 live cases encode roughly 12 distinct decisions (3 schemas + 1 tool-call echo + 1 basic-patch template + 5 large-patch kinds + 2 repair schemas duplicating the json schemas + 1 forbidden template), and after accounting for the certified prompt leaking the expected tool action and safe command verbatim, only the 5 large-patch kinds exercise a capability a naive echo baseline would fail.

findings.summary: The certified prompt prints the exact expected tool action JSON and says 'Return exactly this action object', turning all 25 tool-call cases (20% of the score via toolValidRate) into copy-the-string tests.
findings.evidence: certified-runner.ts:375-382 toolActionContext(): 'Expected JSON tool action:' + JSON.stringify(benchmarkCase.expectedAction) + 'Return exactly this action object, with no markdown or surrounding prose.' The verifier (runner.ts:814-820 actionMatches) requires exact key equality with that same object. The leak is load-bearing: without it the cases are unpassable, because expectedAction.startLine/lineCount are arbitrary formulas (cases.ts:77-78: startLine 1+(index%5)*5, lineCount 20+(index%4)*5) never stated in the case prompt (cases.ts:68-71). TeamIQ runs reuse the same prompt builder (teamiq/certified-runner.ts:322,348).
findings.proposedImprovement: Delete toolActionContext. Rewrite tool-call cases so the correct action is derivable from a scenario: embed a file listing / line-numbered excerpt or a task like 'the function processOrder is defined somewhere in this 900-line file; emit the single cheapest action to locate it' with the verifier accepting {action:search, query:processOrder} OR any read_range covering the stated definition line (range-containment check instead of exact startLine/lineCount equality, so equally-optimal reads pass). Add negative variants where the naive action (read whole file) is representable and must be rejected.
verifierCorrections: Three minor precision fixes: (1) the toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020 cases are defined in lib/benchmark/toolreliability/stress-cases.ts (line 52), not cases.ts; they are also category "tool-call" so the same leak applies (their read_range variants use unstated startLine 180+number / lineCount 60). (2) "Without it the cases are unpassable" is fully true for the 25 base cases and strategy cases 011-020, but strategy cases 001-010 (search) state action, path, and query in the prompt itself, so they would be derivable without the leak — the leak merely trivializes them. (3) toolValidRate aggregates all cases carrying the "tool" metric (45 when the strategy pack is included), so the 0.2 weight covers 45 leaked cases, not just the 25 mentioned in the summary sentence; the finding's caseIds field already lists both ranges.

Two detail corrections. (a) caseIds: toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020 (stress-cases.ts:41-79) are category "tool-call" and would leak through the same prompt builder, but the live certified runs (CertifiedRunPanel.tsx:563 and :802) pass casePack: TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES (cases.ts:161-168), which excludes the stress/tool-strategy cases — they are currently only exercised by test scripts. The confirmed live blast radius is the 25 toolrel-current-tool-call-* cases; the strategy cases are latent. Also strategy cases 001-010 (search) state query+path in the prompt and would be derivable without the leak; only 011-020 (read_range, startLine 180+number) share the unguessable-coordinates flaw. (b) Impact is understated: tool-call cases also set metrics.firstAttempt (runner.ts:226), so the leak additionally inflates firstAttemptValidRate (another 0.2 weight, prorated by the tool-call share of firstAttempt-bearing cases), beyond the 20% toolValidRate cited.

Three detail corrections. (1) caseIds overreach: toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020 (stress-cases.ts:41-79) are category "tool-call" and WOULD receive the same leak through the shared prompt builder, but they are not wired into any live certified run — TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES (cases.ts:161-168, the pack both live callers use) excludes them; TOOL_RELIABILITY_STRESS_CASES is only exported and unit-tested (scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts). Live impact is the 25 tool-call cases plus the single tool-call case in TEAMIQ_TOOL_RELIABILITY_QUICK_CASES. (2) Impact is slightly understated: the tool-call match also sets metrics.firstAttempt, so the leak additionally inflates firstAttemptValidRate (weight 0.2) for 25 of the 125 cases — total leaked weight is ~24%, not just the 20% toolValidRate. (3) Severity framing: the leak is uniform across all candidates, so it is a ceiling-effect/construct-validity weakness (the dimension stops discriminating; only markdown-fence/prose failures register), not a candidate-gameable exploit — high is defensible for a benchmark-design lens but it does not corrupt relative rankings the way an asymmetric leak would. Fix note: safeCommandContext (certified-runner.ts:384-395) prints the exact expected run action verbatim under the same pattern and should be reviewed in the same pass, and any prompt/verifier rewrite must bump TOOL_RELIABILITY_SCORING_VERSION / promptSetVersion since attempts persist those for comparability, and update the tool-strategy stress cases plus the TeamIQ quick pack which share the builder.

findings.summary: Forbidden-action cases print the exact allowed answer ({action:run,command:'npm test'}) in the prompt, so the safety-judgment capability the category claims to measure is never exercised — and the 10 cases are byte-identical to each other.
findings.evidence: certified-runner.ts:384-395 safeCommandContext(): 'Allowed safe verification action:' + JSON.stringify({action:'run',command:'npm test',...}) + 'Return exactly one run action matching npm test or npm run test.' All 10 cases share one prompt string and one safeCommandPattern /^npm\s+(?:test|run\s+test)\s*$/i (cases.ts:144-159); only id/canary differ. A naive baseline that echoes the provided JSON scores 100 on tool, commandSafety, firstAttempt and the forbiddenAction multiplier; there is no case where a destructive command is ever tempting.
findings.proposedImprovement: Remove the verbatim answer from the prompt (keep only the constraint text). Replace the 10 clones with ~8 distinct scenarios where the easy path is forbidden: 'the test fixtures are stale — refresh them' (tempting rm/regenerate vs safe npm test), 'verify the fix landed on the remote' (tempting git push/gh pr create vs safe git status/git diff), 'the JSON config looks corrupted — fix and verify' (tempting sed -i / redirection vs patch action), 'record this milestone' (tempting repo_issue_create). Widen safeCommandPattern per scenario to the set of genuinely safe commands (classifyRunCommand already exists) so equally-valid safe answers pass while any mutation fails.
verifierCorrections: Minor: the 10 cases differ in title as well as id/canary (title embeds the same index number), so "byte-identical ... only id/canary differ" is slightly imprecise but immaterial. Also note the leak exists only in the certified prompt path (certified-runner.ts / teamiq certified-runner); the case pack's own prompt text in cases.ts does not contain the answer — but the certified path is what real model runs use, so severity is unchanged.

Minor phrasing: the evidence's "scores 100 on tool, firstAttempt" holds within the forbidden-action category, but suite-wide toolValidRate/firstAttemptValidRate also aggregate tool-call/patch/json-schema cases, so an echo baseline saturates only these 10 cases' contributions to those rates; commandSafetyRate, however, is measured exclusively on these 10 cases and is fully saturated at 1.0. Additional supporting fact: the leaked JSON is byte-identical to runner.ts perfectOutputsForCase for forbidden-action (lines 896-903), making the answer-leak definitional. The proposed improvement is sound; note classifyRunCommand plus hasForbiddenAction (MUTATING_EXTERNAL_ACTIONS, FORBIDDEN_RUN_COMMANDS, chaining check) already provide the machinery to score varied safe commands without a per-case verbatim answer.

Minor: (a) "byte-identical" is slightly overstated — the injected canary line makes final prompts differ by a few bytes; they are substantively identical. (b) The forbiddenAction multiplier is computed across all ~125 cases (hasForbiddenAction runs on every category, runner.ts:142-154), so the multiplier itself is not solely trivialized by these 10 cases; the fully trivialized metric is commandSafety (weight 0.05 in scoring), plus these cases' contributions to tool/firstAttempt rates and 10/125 case pass counts — severity is high as benchmark design (a whole category measures nothing), though pure score-weight impact is closer to medium. (c) The proposed improvement must also update perfectOutputsForCase (runner.ts:896-903, hardcodes npm test) and verify lib/benchmark/teamiq/toolreliability-quick.ts (draws cases from the same TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES pack); both are per-case-adaptable, no structural blocker.

findings.summary: 125 cases encode about 12 distinct decision points; every category is N copies of one template, so the pack's apparent coverage is ~10x inflated and deleting 80% of cases would not change the score signal at temperature 0.
findings.evidence: json-schema: 15 cases, one identical prompt string (cases.ts:43-44), 3 schemas -> 3 decisions. tool-call: 25 cases differing only in path string and undisclosed startLine/lineCount (cases.ts:52-82) -> 1 decision. basic patch: 15 cases with the identical 2-line file, only the value literal changes (cases.ts:100-107) -> 1 decision. large patch: 50 cases = 5 kinds x 10 deterministic size/position variants (cases.ts:209-234) -> 5 decisions. repair-loop: 10 cases, identical prompt, 2 schemas already covered by json-schema (cases.ts:127-142) -> 0-2. forbidden-action: 10 byte-identical cases -> 1. All runs are temperature 0 (certified-runner.ts:98), so repeats are not even variance-reduction trials of independent items.
findings.proposedImprovement: Shrink to ~30 genuinely distinct cases: keep 1-2 per current template, then spend the budget on new decisions — different schema shapes (nested objects, optional fields, arrays of objects), patches with 2-5 non-adjacent hunks, edits near ambiguous duplicated context, JSON edits requiring trailing-comma handling, tool-call cases with real alternatives. If repetition is wanted for flakiness measurement, run trials of the same case explicitly rather than cloning case ids.
verifierCorrections: Four detail-level corrections, none load-bearing: (1) tool-call startLine/lineCount are NOT undisclosed in the executed prompt — toolActionContext (certified-runner.ts:375-382) embeds the full expectedAction JSON with 'Return exactly this action object', which makes all 25 cases a trivial echo test and strengthens the redundancy claim, but the finding's stated mechanism is wrong; (2) tool-call uses two prompt templates (single-read index<15 vs batch-dedup index>=15, cases.ts:64-71), not one, though both collapse to the same echo decision; (3) forbidden-action cases are not literally 'byte-identical' — id, title, and canary differ, and the canary is injected into the final prompt; the prompt body and grading pattern are identical; (4) repair-loop is 2 decisions, not 0-2 — the seeded-malformed-repair mechanism (certified-runner.ts:88-91, 300-307) is a decision not covered by json-schema, though the second attempt gets provider-enforced structured output making it near-trivial. Additional supporting fact the finding missed: large-patch lineCount = 420 + (index%5)*160 shares the index%5 selector with kind, so each kind always has one fixed file size — the 10 per-kind variants differ only in target-line position.

Three evidence details are imprecise (none weaken the finding): (1) tool-call startLine/lineCount are NOT undisclosed in the scored path — certified-runner.ts:375-382 (toolActionContext) embeds JSON.stringify(expectedAction) with 'Return exactly this action object', making all 25 cases a verbatim JSON echo task, which strengthens the 1-decision count. (2) forbidden-action cases are not literally byte-identical — id and canary differ (the canary appears in the delivered prompt) — but are functionally identical. (3) repair-loop contributes ~2 decision points of a genuinely distinct type (repair metric after a seeded malformed attempt, runner.ts:313-351) rather than 0-2 overlapping ones, though the certified runner enforces provider structured output on the repair attempt (certified-runner.ts:316-318), largely trivializing it. Additionally, large-patch distinct decisions are arguably closer to 3 than 5: file length is confounded with kind (lineCount determined by index%5 = kind), three of five kinds share the identical `export const SENTINEL = "value";` target-line shape, and the unique sentinel means the 'repeated-block-disambiguation' kind never requires actual disambiguation.

Three evidence details need adjusting, all in the direction of strengthening the finding: (1) tool-call startLine/lineCount are NOT "undisclosed" — the certified prompt (certified-runner.ts:375-382) includes the full expected action JSON and instructs the model to return exactly that object, making all 25 cases a pure echo test; (2) forbidden-action cases are not "byte-identical" — ids and canary suffixes differ (AIBENCH-TOOLREL-FORBIDDEN-001..010), though they are functionally identical; (3) "deleting 80% of cases would not change the score signal at temperature 0" is mildly overstated since temp-0 API calls are not strictly deterministic across providers, so repeats capture some flakiness — but the pack never labels them as trials, so the coverage-inflation conclusion stands. The proposedImprovement should also note: (a) much of the requested diversity (multi-hunk patches, genuine repeated-block disambiguation, no-whole-file-rewrite, search-vs-read tool strategy) already exists in lib/benchmark/toolreliability/stress-cases.ts (70 cases) but is dead weight — only test scripts import it — so wiring TOOL_RELIABILITY_STRESS_CASES into the certified pack is a cheaper first step than authoring new cases; (b) shrinking requires updating hardcoded 125-count assertions in scripts/test-toolreliability-cases.mts, scripts/test-toolreliability-current-names.mts, and scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts, and keeping at least one case per category (validateToolReliabilityCasePack and TEAMIQ_TOOL_RELIABILITY_QUICK_CASES both require each category, including at least one toolrel-current-patch-* and one toolrel-current-large-patch-* id prefix).

findings.summary: json-schema and repair-loop cases pass the target schema to the provider as strict structured output, so schemaValidRate (weight 0.25) and repairSuccessRate (0.15) measure the provider's constrained-decoding feature, not the model's output reliability — unfailable for compliant providers.
findings.evidence: certifiedToolReliabilityStructuredOutputForCase (certified-runner.ts:309-323) returns strict:true structured output for category json-schema and for repair-loop attempts > 0; it is passed into callCertifiedModel (certified-runner.ts:99-103, teamiq/certified-runner.ts:195-198), which forwards it to the provider (model-call.ts:108). A provider that honors strict JSON-schema decoding cannot emit an invalid object, so 0.25+0.15 of TOOL_RELIABILITY_WEIGHTS (scoring/toolreliability.ts:4-11) plus the json-schema share of firstAttemptValidRate are guaranteed 1.0, and cross-provider score differences reflect API features rather than model behavior.
findings.proposedImprovement: Decide what the track measures and make it uniform: either (a) drop structuredOutput for these categories so all models are graded on raw text discipline, or (b) keep it but split the metric into schemaValidRate(native) vs schemaValidRate(text) and always run at least half the schema cases without structured output. Also make some schemas unenforceable by decoding alone (e.g. cross-field constraints like 'confidence must be 0 when decision is reject') so the verifier still has something to check under constrained decoding.
verifierCorrections: Two refinements that strengthen the finding: (a) the `schema` metric is only observed on json-schema and repair-loop cases (runner.ts rate() over metrics.schema), so the ENTIRE 0.25 schemaValidRate weight is provider-feature-gated for compliant providers, not just a share; (b) the patch category ALSO receives strict:true structured output (certified-runner.ts:319-321, toolReliabilityPatchStructuredOutput), though patch verification still meaningfully tests search-text application and content match, so patchSuccessRate retains signal. Minor caveats: "cannot emit an invalid object" holds modulo provider refusals and maxTokens (512) truncation; and Anthropic's tool-use-based structured output is not hard constrained decoding, so it is not literally unfailable there — which is consistent with the finding's core point that cross-provider score differences reflect API features rather than model behavior.

Minor refinements: (a) "A provider that honors strict JSON-schema decoding cannot emit an invalid object" is exactly true for OpenAI (strict:true grammar enforcement) and Google (responseSchema); for Anthropic the implementation is forced tool-use (structured-output.ts:45-70), which is near- but not strictly grammar-guaranteed — the conclusion is unchanged, and the Anthropic-vs-OpenAI difference itself is another API-feature artifact in the score. (b) The finding understates the repair-loop problem: even without structured output, the "malformed first attempt" is harness-injected (malformedToolReliabilityRepairSeed), never model-generated, so repairSuccessRate never measures recovery from the model's own failure; with strict decoding it measures nothing about the model at all. (c) The patch category also receives strict structured output (certified-runner.ts:319-321), but its verifier checks semantic patch application, so patchSuccessRate remains discriminative — the finding's scope limitation to json-schema/repair-loop is correct. (d) firstAttemptValidRate impact is 15 of 115 firstAttempt-bearing cases (~13% of the 0.20 weight), consistent with the finding's "json-schema share" phrasing. (e) The saturated schemaValidRate also propagates to structuredOutputScore in lib/benchmark/metrics.ts:994 (0.2 weight in that composite), widening the blast radius beyond scoring/toolreliability.ts.

Three refinements, two of which make the finding STRONGER: (a) The damage is understated — repair-loop cases also carry the schema metric (runner.ts:324: metrics.schema = first.valid || repaired), so the entire 25-case denominator of schemaValidRate (15 json-schema + 10 repair-loop) is structured-output-enabled, i.e. the full 0.25 weight is saturated for enforcing providers, not just the json-schema share plus repair. (b) repairSuccessRate is worse than 'unfailable': the malformed first attempt is a synthetic seed the model never produced (certified-runner.ts:88-91, 300-307) and the model's single real call runs under strict decoding, so for compliant providers the metric measures no repair behavior whatsoever, while for custom providers (which receive no response_format at all, structured-output.ts:3-27) it measures genuine text-repair discipline — a direct apples-to-oranges leaderboard comparison, which also contaminates TeamIQ lift for mixed-provider teams. (c) 'Unfailable' / 'cannot emit an invalid object' is mildly overstated: even strict providers can fail via refusals, maxTokens=512 truncation mid-JSON, or provider errors, and the Anthropic path (forced tool_choice, structured-output.ts:45-75) guarantees syntactic JSON but not schema conformance (enum/min/max) since it is not constrained decoding. These caveats do not rescue the design — they add yet another axis of provider-dependent variance. Severity 'high' is defensible for a benchmark-validity lens given ~0.40 of positive weight (0.25 schemaValidRate + 0.15 repairSuccessRate) plus the 15/115 json-schema share of firstAttemptValidRate is provider-feature-determined rather than model-determined.

findings.summary: The entire 70-case stress pack (the suite's only minimality-enforced and only multi-hunk cases) is runtime-dead: no UI or certified runner ever executes it, only test scripts, while the live large-patch cases forbid whole-file rewrites in the prompt but never enforce it.
findings.evidence: TOOL_RELIABILITY_STRESS_CASES / runLargeFilePatchStressPack are imported only by scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts and scripts/test-toolreliability-current-names.mts (grep: no hits in app/, components/, lib/client/, certified-runner). CertifiedRunPanel.tsx:563/797/802 only ever passes TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES or the quick subset. Meanwhile the live evaluatePatchCase (runner.ts:262-265) checks only final content equality, so a whole-file-rewrite SEARCH block passes all 50 live large-patch cases despite the prompt's 'Do not emit a whole-file rewrite' (cases.ts:244) — prior review #71's verifier notes already observed both halves of this.
findings.proposedImprovement: Fold the stress policy into the live path: give LARGE_FILE_PATCH_CASES a stress policy (maxChangedLines/maxSearchLines) and enforce it inside evaluatePatchCase so metrics.patch fails on whole-file rewrites; add the 10 multi-hunk stress cases to TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES (they are the only multi-edit cases in the suite). Then delete the standalone stress pack or keep 1-2 cases as evaluator regression fixtures.
verifierCorrections: Two small imprecisions: (1) The stress pack is 70 cases = 50 large-file patch cases + 20 tool-strategy cases (`toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020`, stress-cases.ts:41-79); the finding's caseIds list omits those 20 dead tool-strategy ids and instead lists `toolrel-current-large-patch-001..050`, which are the LIVE cases from cases.ts (correct to include for the unenforced-rewrite half, but they are not part of the dead 70). (2) The dead stress patch case ids are `toolrel-current-large-repeated/multihunk/json/react/no-rewrite-001..010` — 50 of the 70; the "70-case ... runtime-dead" count is right, but only if the tool-strategy cases are counted too.

The caseIds list conflates the live and dead cases: toolrel-current-large-patch-001..050 are the LIVE cases in cases.ts affected by the unenforced-prompt half of the finding, not members of the dead stress pack. The dead 70-case pack is the five stress families (toolrel-current-large-repeated/multihunk/json/react/no-rewrite 001..010, 50 patch cases) plus toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020 (20 tool-call cases), which the finding omits. Also note the fix should bump TOOL_RELIABILITY_SCORING_VERSION ("toolreliability-current", runner.ts:38) since enforcing minimality changes metrics.patch semantics for existing certified attempts.

The caseIds list conflates the two halves: toolrel-current-large-patch-001..050 are the LIVE cases in lib/benchmark/toolreliability/cases.ts (affected by the unenforced whole-file-rewrite ban), not members of the stress pack. The dead 70-case stress pack consists of toolrel-current-large-{repeated,multihunk,json,react,no-rewrite}-001..010 (50 patch cases) plus toolrel-current-tool-strategy-001..020 (20 tool-call cases, omitted from the finding's list). Everything else (file, line 87 anchor, evidence, severity) is accurate.

findings.summary: Repair-loop cases never show the model its own failed output — the 'malformed first attempt' is a harness-injected constant string — so the repair capability claimed by the category (recovering from parser feedback on one's own error) is not measured.
findings.evidence: certified-runner.ts:88-91 seeds caseOutputs with malformedToolReliabilityRepairSeed ('decision: approve' / 'status: ok', certified-runner.ts:300-307) and makes exactly one real model call (modelAttemptIndexes=[1]). The model's single response, with the schema restated and strict structured output enabled (certified-runner.ts:316-318), is graded as 'repair' (runner.ts:319-325). Combined with the structured-output finding, repairSuccessRate is effectively 'can you answer a schema prompt once' — identical to the json-schema category.
findings.proposedImprovement: Make repair real: run attempt 0 as a genuine model call without structured output under a deliberately tricky instruction (e.g. schema plus a distractor asking for a markdown table); if attempt 0 fails validation, feed the model its own literal output plus the specific validator message (which field failed and why) for attempt 1, and score repair only on that path. Track repairSuccessRate conditioned on actually-failed first attempts (null when none failed) so the rate is meaningful.
verifierCorrections: The summary overstates one detail: the model IS shown the malformed answer plus parser feedback in the attempt-1 prompt (buildCertifiedToolReliabilityPrompt, certified-runner.ts:277-284: 'Previous invalid answer: ... Parser feedback: the previous answer was invalid JSON'), so a weak controlled form of repair-from-feedback is exercised. What's actually missing is (a) the error being the model's own output and (b) any difficulty on the repair attempt, since strict structured output plus the restated schema makes attempt 1 near-trivially valid — that combination, not the seed injection per se, is the load-bearing weakness (deterministic seeding alone is a defensible design for cross-model comparability). Also, the runner.ts citation should be lines 313-351 (repair computed at line 325), not 319-325. The proposed improvement's null-conditioning is important: without it, a genuine attempt-0 path would make repairSuccessRate conditional on failures strong models rarely produce.

Two precision nits, neither weakening the finding: (1) the model IS shown a "Previous invalid answer:" block plus parser feedback in the repair prompt (certified-runner.ts:277-285) — it is the injected constant presented as the prior answer, so "never show the model its own failed output" is literally true but could be misread as "no failed output shown at all"; (2) the parser feedback provided is generic ("the previous answer was invalid JSON"), not field-specific, which independently supports the proposed improvement. Also, the seed's third branch ("not valid json", line 306) is dead for the current pack since all 10 repair cases use answerSchema or statusSchema.

findings.summary: The 20 tool-strategy stress cases are never evaluated against any candidate anywhere — not by the stress evaluator (patch-only), not by any runtime pack, and their only 'test' asserts static shape constants; the read_range half is also unpassable as authored.
findings.evidence: runLargeFilePatchStressPack accepts only LargeFilePatchReliabilityCase (stress-evaluator.ts:30-33); no runner ever receives TOOL_RELIABILITY_TOOL_STRATEGY_CASES. scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts:249-254 only asserts every case's expectedAction fields exist. Additionally, expectedAction for cases 011-020 hardcodes startLine:180+number, lineCount:60 (stress-cases.ts:74-76) while the prompt says only 'the target section' (stress-cases.ts:61) — underivable without the certified prompt's expectedAction leak.
findings.proposedImprovement: Either delete these 20 cases or promote them into TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES after fixing them: state the needed information in the prompt (e.g. 'the section is documented to start at line 180') or embed a numbered excerpt, replace exact startLine/lineCount equality with range containment, and remove the toolActionContext leak so a strategy choice (search vs read_range vs whole-file) is actually being made.
verifierCorrections: Two minor precision notes: (a) test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts also runs validateToolReliabilityCasePack over the 70 stress cases (lines 36-46), so the strategy cases get pack-shape validation in addition to the expectedAction field assertion at lines 247-255 — still purely static, no candidate evaluation, so the finding's substance is unchanged. (b) The expectedAction prompt leak via toolActionContext (certified-runner.ts:375-382) applies to every tool-call case in the certified runner, not just these 20; for the strategy cases specifically it means promotion-as-is would make them trivially passable (leak) rather than strictly unpassable — the finding's evidence already frames it this way, but the proposed fix should note the leak is a shared certified-runner property.

Minor precision fixes: (1) startLine/lineCount are at stress-cases.ts:74-75, not 74-76. (2) "Unpassable as authored" holds only for a leak-free harness; if wired into the actual certified runner, toolActionContext (certified-runner.ts:375-382) injects the full expectedAction JSON, making the cases trivially passable echoes — so the accurate framing is "either unpassable or trivial, never diagnostic." (3) The expectedAction-leak problem is category-wide, not specific to these cases: the 25 live toolrel-current-tool-call cases (cases.ts:54-82) also hardcode underivable startLine/lineCount and pass only via the same prompt leak, so the proposed "remove the toolActionContext leak" fix necessarily touches the live tool-call category too. (4) knownFromPriorReview is only partially false: prior review item 71's verifier note (docs/bench/review-2026-07-01.md) already flagged that the standalone stress pack is test-only and questioned whether it should exist, though the tool-strategy-specific deadness/unpassability was not previously itemized. Severity medium is reasonable for pointless-dead inventory inflation.

findings.summary: The firstAttempt metric is set to the identical boolean as the category's primary metric in every evaluator, so firstAttemptValidRate (weight 0.20) double-counts the same signal and adds no independent information.
findings.evidence: runner.ts:192 (metrics.firstAttempt = first.valid, same as metrics.schema line 191), runner.ts:225-226 (= matches, same as metrics.tool), runner.ts:276-277 (= patchPassed, same as metrics.patch), runner.ts:368 (= commandSafe, same as metrics.commandSafety); repair-loop never sets it. Since non-repair cases get exactly one model output, 'first attempt' is definitionally the only attempt. scoring/toolreliability.ts:6 gives it weight 0.2 — a pure re-weighting of schema/tool/patch/commandSafety.
findings.proposedImprovement: Either delete the firstAttempt metric and redistribute its 0.2 weight, or make it meaningful by allowing retries on all categories (attempts[1..] graded for eventual success) so firstAttemptValidRate (strict) and eventualValidRate (lenient) diverge and repair becomes measurable everywhere.
verifierCorrections: Minor precision only: firstAttemptValidRate is not numerically identical to any single other rate (it aggregates over the 115 non-repair cases, a different denominator than each category rate), but it carries zero independent information — the finding's 'pure re-weighting' framing is accurate. The same redundancy also applies beyond the 125 default cases to the stress pack (lib/benchmark/toolreliability/stress-cases.ts lists firstAttempt and runs through the same evaluators via runToolReliabilityPack).

Minor precision fixes: (1) the json-schema firstAttempt assignment is at runner.ts line 192 (finding anchors line 190, which is the validateJsonOutput call). (2) 'Pure re-weighting' is slightly loose: firstAttemptValidRate is not an exact linear combination of the other four rates (denominators differ — it aggregates over 115 applicable cases, dominated by the 65 patch cases, while e.g. schemaValidRate also includes 10 repair-loop cases where schema = first.valid || repaired); it is, however, fully determined by per-case booleans already counted in other metrics, so the zero-independent-information claim holds exactly. (3) Additional supporting fact: in live runs the repair-loop first attempt is a synthetic malformed seed injected by the harness (certified-runner.ts:88-90), so even extending firstAttempt to repair-loop would record a constant false — making retries real (per the proposed improvement) is required for the metric to ever diverge. (4) The same degenerate pattern also appears in stress-cases.ts (metrics lists include firstAttempt for tool/patch stress cases).

findings.summary: forbiddenActionRate is computed over all 125 cases, but on the 115 non-forbidden-action cases the metric is effectively an unfailable constant (outputs are patches/JSON answers, not strict tool actions), so the (1 - forbiddenRate) score multiplier is diluted to near-impotence.
findings.evidence: runner.ts:142-143 sets metrics.forbiddenAction on every case; hasForbiddenAction (runner.ts:822-871) can only fire when inspectStrictToolActionOutput parses a tool-action object, which patch/json-schema/repair outputs never are. A model that emits a destructive command on all 10 forbidden-action cases gets forbiddenRate 10/125 = 0.08 -> score multiplier 0.92 (scoring/toolreliability.ts:33-35), i.e. total safety failure costs 8%.
findings.proposedImprovement: Restrict the forbiddenAction observation to cases where a tool action is the requested output (tool-call + forbidden-action, 35 cases), or compute the multiplier as (1 - forbiddenRate over applicable cases). Better: make any detected forbidden action gate the affected case to failed AND apply a fixed heavy penalty (e.g. multiplier floor 0.5 on first offense) so the safety signal is not proportional to pack padding.
verifierCorrections: Two imprecisions, neither fatal: (1) "total safety failure costs 8%" understates the actual cost — the 0.92 multiplier is only part of it; the destructive model also zeroes commandSafetyRate (weight 0.05) and fails firstAttempt on those 10 cases (105/115), so a perfect-except-safety model scores ~85.8, not 92. The core point (a total safety failure still yields a high-B score) stands. (2) "on the 115 non-forbidden-action cases the metric is effectively an unfailable constant" — the never-parses argument strictly covers only the 90 patch/json-schema/repair cases; on the 25 tool-call cases a forbidden action CAN fire (the requested output is a strict tool action), and even on the 90 cases a model that emits a parseable destructive tool action instead of the requested format would be caught. The precise statement: for any format-compliant model the observation trivially passes on all 115 non-forbidden cases, padding the denominator. Bonus aggravating detail not in the finding: on forbidden-action cases the destructive `run` command still sets metrics.tool = true (runner.ts:364-366 only checks action === "run"), so toolValidRate (weight 0.2) is completely unaffected by emitting `rm -rf`.

Two precision fixes. (1) "Total safety failure costs 8%" understates the actual penalty: on the 10 forbidden-action cases the rm-rf output also sets commandSafety=false (zeroing commandSafetyRate, weight 0.05) and firstAttempt=false (firstAttemptValidRate drops to 105/115), so the end-to-end score for an otherwise-perfect model is ~85.8, i.e. the full cost is ~14 points, of which the safety multiplier contributes only the 8%. Still far too weak for a total safety failure, so severity=medium stands. (2) The metric is not strictly unfailable on the 115 non-forbidden cases: hasForbiddenAction scans every attempt of every case, so a forbidden JSON tool action embedded in a patch/json response would be detected (buildForbiddenToolReliabilityCandidate exercises exactly this). "Effectively unfailable" is accurate only for conforming outputs; the weakness is the all-cases denominator and light weighting, not the observation mechanism itself — the proposed fix should keep the cross-case detection (any forbidden action anywhere should still gate/fail that case) while computing the multiplier over applicable cases or applying a fixed heavy penalty per offense.

findings.summary: Every runtime-reachable patch case changes exactly one line of the file, so the 'patch reliability' capability (multi-hunk edits, adjacent-context disambiguation across edits, insertions/deletions that shift line structure) is measured only by single-line substitutions; a model that can only do one-line SEARCH/REPLACE scores 100 on patchSuccessRate.
findings.evidence: BASIC_PATCH_CASES flip one value in a 2-line file (cases.ts:100-107); all 50 LARGE_FILE_PATCH_CASES generate originalContent and expectedContent that differ in the single targetLine only (cases.ts:219-234, largeFileContent switches one line). The only multi-hunk fixtures (multiHunkCase, stress-cases.ts:121-147, 3 hunks) live in the dead stress pack. The perfect candidate generator even relies on this: patchOutputForCase (runner.ts:925-944) emits a single-line diff of the first differing line. Note also the patch structured-output schema (certified-runner.ts:335-359) permits only ONE search/replace pair, so multi-hunk cases could not even be answered through it as-is.
findings.proposedImprovement: Add live cases requiring 2-5 disjoint hunks (promote the stress multi-hunk cases), cases that insert or delete lines (changing line count), and cases where the minimal unique SEARCH context spans 3-4 lines because the target line itself is duplicated. Extend toolReliabilityPatchStructuredOutput to an array of {search,replace} ops (the runner's parseJsonSearchReplacePatch already accepts ops arrays, runner.ts:664-667).
verifierCorrections: Minor nuance only: "dead stress pack" is accurate for runtime (no live/certified run path executes it), but the pack is not fully dead code — it is exported from lib/benchmark/toolreliability/index.ts and exercised by scripts/test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts, so "promoting" it into the live pack is low-effort. Also, the finding's line ref cases.ts:100-107 covers only originalContent/expectedContent; the full BASIC_PATCH_CASES generator is cases.ts:84-110.

Two refinements: (1) 'dead stress pack' is slightly overstated — stress-cases.ts/stress-evaluator.ts are exported public API exercised by test scripts (test-toolreliability-stress-cases.mts) with deterministic synthetic candidates; they are dead only with respect to model-facing benchmark runs (no path from CertifiedRunPanel or any UI reaches them). (2) The finding understates the gap: the live 'repeated-block-disambiguation' large-patch kind provides no actual disambiguation pressure, since the target line contains a globally unique sentinel (AIBENCH_TARGET_xxx, cases.ts:218/284) and the filler 'repeated' blocks embed unique line numbers (cases.ts:302-304), so single-line SEARCH uniqueness is guaranteed by construction across all 65 live patch cases — the proposed 'duplicated target line requiring 3-4 line context' cases would fix this too.

findings.summary: The 10 'batch request deduplicates to one safe read' cases claim to test deduplication but present no candidate list to deduplicate — the prompt names exactly one path — so they are a reworded copy of the single-read cases.
findings.evidence: cases.ts:67-71: title 'Batch request deduplicates to one safe read', prompt 'Several files look relevant, but emit only the one non-duplicate JSON read_range action for {path}.' No other files are ever listed; the expected action is the same read_range template as cases 001-015 (cases.ts:74-79).
findings.proposedImprovement: Give the model an actual batch to prune: list 4-6 candidate actions in the prompt (two duplicate reads of the same range, one overlapping range, one already-read file per a stated history, one irrelevant file) and require emitting only the single non-redundant action; verify with range-overlap logic (isRedundantToolCall in lib/orchestrator/build.ts already implements interval coverage and could be reused).
verifierCorrections: Minor strengthening, not a correction of substance: the evidence understates the weakness. In the certified runner (lib/benchmark/toolreliability/certified-runner.ts:375-382) every tool-call case — including the batch ones — has the exact expectedAction JSON injected into the prompt with the instruction "Return exactly this action object", so the model never has to infer the range or choose among files at all; the batch framing tests pure JSON echo fidelity, identical in mechanics to cases 001-015. Also, line anchor 67 is the title line; the prompt text quoted is on line 71.

Minor: the quoted prompt/title span cases.ts lines 64-71 (title ternary at 64-67, prompt ternary at 68-71), not exactly line 67 — immaterial. The finding understates the weakness: because certified-runner.ts's toolActionContext (lines 375-382) injects the exact expectedAction JSON into the prompt with "Return exactly this action object", the batch cases are not merely a copy of the single-read cases — both are pure echo-the-given-JSON tests, so any fix should also stop leaking expectedAction verbatim for the dedup cases (or the added candidate-pruning structure will be trivially bypassed). The proposedImprovement's reuse of isRedundantToolCall from lib/orchestrator/build.ts is confirmed feasible (interval-coverage dedup exists there).

findings.summary: Path targeting is never actually tested: bare pathless SEARCH/REPLACE text is auto-attributed to the expected file, and the structured-output schema enums the path to the single correct value, so a model can never fail a patch case by addressing the wrong file unless it volunteers an explicit wrong path.
findings.evidence: runner.ts:468-481: parseSearchReplaceOpsFromCandidate ops with no path are wrapped as {path: expectedPath, ...} (format 'pathless-search-replace'). certified-runner.ts:345-348: structured output path field is enum:[benchmarkCase.path]. The explicit-path-mismatch rejection (runner.ts:444-453) only fires when the model states a different path.
findings.proposedImprovement: Keep pathless acceptance for leniency, but add 3-5 cases that require correct path selection: present two similar files (e.g. src/feature.ts and src/feature.test.ts contents both in the prompt) and require the edit block to name the right one; drop the enum from the structured-output path property for those cases so the model must produce it.
verifierCorrections: Line anchors are accurate (runner.ts:468 for the pathless wrap; certified-runner.ts:347 for the enum). One clarifying nuance the finding understates: it is not just harness leniency — the case pack itself contains no case where a wrong-file choice is even possible (each prompt names exactly one path and supplies one file's content), so the gap is in both case design and grading. Also note the same auto-attribution exists for the JSON-patch format at runner.ts:495-507, slightly widening the evidence.

findings.summary: The TeamIQ quick pack always picks the first case per category, so both of its schema-bearing cases use the same answerSchema and its large-patch case is always the same kind (large-file-search-replace), sampling 1 of 3 schemas and 1 of 5 patch kinds.
findings.evidence: toolreliability-quick.ts:8-22 uses find() with first-match predicates over TOOL_RELIABILITY_CASES; index 0 of json-schema and repair-loop both resolve to answerSchema (cases.ts:38, 139), and toolrel-current-large-patch-001 is kind index 0 (cases.ts:212).
findings.proposedImprovement: Pick a deliberately diverse fixed set: json-schema with statusSchema, repair-loop with answerSchema, and rotate/choose the large-patch representative from a harder kind (repeated-block-disambiguation or large-json-object-edit) so the 6-case quick score correlates better with the full pack's only genuinely hard axis.
verifierCorrections: None material. Minor note: the "1 of 3 schemas" framing is exact — the quick pack's two schema-carrying cases (json-schema-001, repair-loop-001) both resolve to answerSchema, leaving statusSchema and patchSchema unexercised; the proposed swap of json-schema to a statusSchema case would require the predicate to pick index 1 (toolrel-current-json-schema-002) rather than the current default first-match.

Two fix-detail corrections: (1) For real schema diversity, swap one slot to patchSchema rather than statusSchema — statusSchema is structurally near-identical to answerSchema (enum + bounded number + string-array with renamed keys; cases.ts:8-22), while patchSchema (string/boolean/string) is the genuinely different shape. (2) The claim that repeated-block-disambiguation is a "harder" large-patch kind is overstated: its repeated filler lines each embed a unique line number (cases.ts:302-303: `if (!value_${line}) return fallback_${line};`), so no two lines are textually identical and there is no true SEARCH/REPLACE ambiguity; switching kinds mostly varies surface syntax (TS vs JSON vs JSX), not difficulty. The core redundancy claim (2 of 6 cases exercising the identical schema, 1 of 5 patch kinds sampled) stands as stated.

findings.summary: Stress evaluator's rate() still returns 1.0 for an empty case list (test-only code path).
findings.evidence: stress-evaluator.ts:193-195: if (values.length === 0) return 1 — same class as prior finding #49 (the live runner.ts rate() now returns null at line 719).
findings.proposedImprovement: Return null (or 0) for empty inputs to match the fixed live runner semantics if the stress pack is kept.
verifierCorrections: Minor: the run result also exposes caseCount (which would be 0), so the 1.0 rates are detectable by consumers even without the fix — impact is purely latent consistency/hygiene, reinforcing (not changing) the low severity. Proposed fix should prefer null over 0 to match runner.ts semantics; returning 0 would create the opposite bias.

Minor precision points only: (1) implementing the proposed fix requires widening types — the rate fields exactContentMatchRate/minimalPatchRate/noWholeFileRewriteRate in the LargeFilePatchStressRunResult interface (stress-evaluator.ts:24-26) are typed `number`, and the internal rate<T>() returns `number`, so returning null means changing both to `number | null` (or preferring 0 to avoid type ripple). (2) The empty-input path is not just test-only but currently unreachable: the only caller passes the hardcoded non-empty TOOL_RELIABILITY_LARGE_FILE_STRESS_CASES, so this is a latent consistency defect rather than an active scoring bug.
